homepage.forecastShows: Latest forecast shows {party} ahead by {margin} points, with a {probability} chance of winning the most seats
homepage.aboutDescription: This election model combines polling data, historical voting patterns, and demographic information to project likely outcomes for Portugal's parliamentary elections. The forecast is updated regularly as new data becomes available.
homepage.districtDescription: Geographic distribution of forecasts by district.

map.title: District Map
map.subtitle: Interactive District Map
map.selectDistrict: Explore election forecasts by district. Click on any district to see the predicted vote percentage for each party.
map.colorsDescription: Each district is colored with the party that has the highest predicted vote percentage in that region.
map.interactionDescription: Hover over a district to see the leading party and percentage, or click to see all details.
map.islandsDescription: The Azores and Madeira appear as multiple islands, but forecasts are aggregated by region.

forecast.title: Full Election Forecast
forecast.subtitle: Portugal Parliamentary Elections
forecast.coalitionDescription: Each dot represents one simulation outcome. Black lines show median seat counts for each coalition.
forecast.simulationDescription: Based on {count} Monte Carlo simulations. Dots show mean projections, bands show 50% and 80% credibility intervals.
forecast.pollingTrendsDescription: Smoothed polling averages based on {count} data points. Lines show estimated vote share over time with uncertainty bands.
forecast.dataSourcesDescription: Polling data from major Portuguese polling firms, weighted by reliability and recency. Historical election results and demographic data.
forecast.methodologyDescription: Monte Carlo simulation accounting for polling uncertainty, district-level variations, and D'Hondt seat allocation system.
forecast.updatesDescription: Model is updated as new polling data becomes available. Uncertainty decreases as election day approaches.

articles.title: Analysis & Articles
articles.subtitle: In-depth analysis of Portuguese politics and election trends
articles.comingSoonDescription: We're working on more in-depth analysis pieces covering Portuguese electoral dynamics, polling trends, and forecast methodology.
articles.newsletterNotification: Subscribe to our newsletter to be notified when new articles are published.

article1.title: Understanding Portugal's Electoral System
article1.excerpt: A comprehensive guide to how Portugal's proportional representation system works and why it matters for forecasting.

article2.title: The Rise of Chega: Analyzing the Right-Wing Surge

article3.title: Polling Accuracy in Portuguese Elections: A Historical Analysis
article3.excerpt: How accurate have Portuguese polls been historically, and what can we learn for future forecasting?

about.title: About estimador.pt
about.subtitle: Independent, data-driven election forecasting for Portugal
about.contact: For questions, suggestions, or media inquiries, please contact us at
about.combiningExpertise: as an independent project combining Bayesian statistics, computational methods, and analysis of Portuguese electoral data.
about.missionDescription1: estimador.pt provides transparent, probabilistic forecasts for Portuguese elections. We believe democratic societies benefit when citizens have access to rigorous, unbiased analysis of electoral data—not just punditry and speculation.
about.missionDescription2: Our goal is to bring the standards of modern election forecasting—pioneered by projects like FiveThirtyEight and The Economist—to the Portuguese context, while adapting our methods to Portugal's multi-party proportional representation system.

whatWeDoList.forecasting: Bayesian models that combine polling data with historical patterns, accounting for uncertainty at every step
whatWeDoList.seatProjections: District-level analysis using the D'Hondt method to project how national vote shares translate to parliamentary seats
whatWeDoList.trendAnalysis: Gaussian Process models that track the evolution of party support over time, separating signal from noise
whatWeDoList.pollingAnalysis: Systematic estimation of pollster house effects—the consistent biases each polling firm shows toward certain parties

about.approachDescription1: We use Bayesian statistical methods that explicitly quantify uncertainty. Unlike point predictions, our forecasts produce probability distributions that reflect what we actually know—and don't know—about election outcomes.

approachPrinciples.transparency: Open methodology with detailed documentation of our models and assumptions
approachPrinciples.independence: No political affiliations, no partisan funding, no editorial pressure
approachPrinciples.accuracy: Continuous validation against actual election results, with honest assessment of forecast errors
approachPrinciples.accessibility: Presenting probabilistic forecasts in ways that general audiences can understand and use

about.limitationsDescription1: Election forecasting is inherently uncertain—that's why we present probabilities, not predictions. Our models account for several sources of uncertainty:

limitationsList.pollingError: Systematic and random polling error, including potential bias not captured by past elections
limitationsList.campaignDynamics: Late-breaking news and campaign events that can shift voter preferences
limitationsList.turnout: Differential turnout patterns that may not match historical patterns
limitationsList.regional: District-level variations that deviate from national trends

about.limitationsDescription2: A forecast showing a party with a 70% chance of winning most seats also means there's a 30% chance they won't. Our forecasts should be interpreted as informed probabilistic assessments, not certainties.
about.footerCopyright: © 2025–2026 estimador.pt • Built with transparency and open methodology

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.subtitle: How our election forecast models work

secondRound.title: Second Round Forecast
secondRound.headline: {candidate} has {probability} chance of winning
secondRound.headlineDescription: Forecast for the presidential runoff election between {candidateA} and {candidateB}.
secondRound.trendDescription: Polling averages with uncertainty intervals for second round candidates.
secondRound.simulationDescription: Each dot represents one possible election outcome based on simulations.

presidential.leadingHeadline: {candidate} has {probability} chance of reaching the runoff
presidential.snapshotHeadline: {candidate} has {probability} chance of reaching the second round
presidential.forecastDescription: Our model shows a {secondRoundProbability} probability of a second round being needed, with no candidate reaching the 50% threshold in the first round.
presidential.snapshotDescription: Based on the latest polls, there is a {secondRoundProbability} probability of a second round being needed.
presidential.snapshotNote: If the election were held today
presidential.metricChangeDescription: We now show each candidate's probability of reaching the second round (sum of all runoff scenarios where they appear), which is more meaningful than first-round leading odds.
presidential.spaghettiDescription: Each line represents one possible simulation outcome. The spread of lines shows the uncertainty in our forecast - with ~35% undecided voters, outcomes remain highly uncertain.
presidential.trendDescription: Estimated support over time for the top candidates. Lines show the mean estimate, shaded bands show 50% and 95% credible intervals. Dots represent individual polls.
presidential.modelDescription: This forecast uses a Bayesian model that combines polling data with candidate-specific priors based on party affiliation. The model accounts for pollster house effects and uncertainty in voter preferences.
presidential.undecidedWarning: Important: Approximately 35% of voters remain undecided. This forecast shows declared voting intention only - actual results may differ significantly as undecided voters make their choices.
presidential.factor2: Candidate party affiliations and historical patterns
presidential.aboutDescription: Portuguese presidential elections use a two-round system. If no candidate receives more than 50% of votes in the first round, the top two candidates proceed to a second round. This forecast models declared voting intention based on available polling data.
presidential.runoffScenariosDescription: With a second round nearly certain, these are the most likely candidate matchups based on our simulations.

assumptions.title: Model Assumptions
assumptions.declared_voters: Forecasts DECLARED voters only (~65% of electorate)
assumptions.house_effects: House effects informed by parliamentary election data

football.modelDescription: We simulated the rest of the season {count} times, varying each match result based on team performance, squad value, and home advantage. The probabilities shown reflect how often each outcome occurs across those simulations.
football.methodologySubtitle: How our Liga Portugal forecast model works
football.methodologyDescription: The liga-predict model uses Bayesian inference to predict the results of remaining Liga Portugal matches. It combines performance data with squad-value-based priors, estimating attack and defense parameters for each team.
football.teamPageDescription: Title scenarios and decisive matches for {team} in Liga Portugal {season}.
football.scenariosDescription: Three possible paths to the title, from most likely to least likely. Each scenario shows what needs to happen — and what rivals must do.
football.survivalScenariosDescription: Three possible paths to avoiding relegation, based on model simulations.
football.decisiveMatchesForDescription: The remaining fixtures with the biggest impact on {team}'s title probability.
football.decisiveMatchesForDescriptionSurvival: The remaining fixtures with the biggest impact on {team}'s survival probability.
football.featuredMatchesForDescription: {team}'s fixtures that influence the title race or relegation battle.
football.probabilityOverTimeDescription: How {target} probability has evolved across matchdays.
football.remainingScheduleDescription: All remaining fixtures with win, draw, and loss probabilities.
football.buildYourPathDescription: Pick results and see how the probability changes match by match.
football.positionDistributionDescription: Probability of finishing in each league position.
football.whatIfDescription: Pick results and see how probabilities change
football.simulatorCta: Pick match results and see the impact on probabilities
football.teamClickHint: Click a team to see detailed scenarios
football.scheduleDifficultyDescription: Average remaining opponent strength based on model parameters.
football.teamStrengthsDescription: Attack and defense parameters estimated by the Bayesian model.
football.pointsPaceDescription: Points projection based on current performance. Solid bar: actual points. Faded bar: projection to 34 matchdays.
football.luckIndexDescription: Expected points (xPts) computed from each match's expected goals (xG). The difference to actual points measures finishing efficiency and luck.
football.magicNumbersDescription: Points needed to mathematically guarantee each objective, regardless of other results.

meta.economicsTitle: State of the economy — Portugal | Estimador
meta.economicsDescription: A consolidated, uncertainty-first read-out of the current state of the Portuguese economy — activity pulse, recession risk, downside risk and a preliminary GDP nowcast. Not a forecast.

economics.title: State of the economy
economics.pageIntro: A faster, consolidated read-out of where the Portuguese economy is now — not a GDP forecast.
economics.healthCaveat: A fixed-rule blend of the activity, recession-risk and downside signals below.
economics.healthBlendNote: {activity}% activity + {downside}% downside, capped by recession risk.
economics.pulseSubtitle: Where activity is running now — anchored on the latest BdP Coincident Activity print.
economics.recessionCurrentCaveat: Where recession risk stands now — a calibrated read available before the GDP print.
economics.recessionLeadingCaveat: Looking two quarters ahead — anticipates gradual downturns, not sudden shocks.
economics.garSubtitle: Range of next-quarter GDP growth outcomes
economics.garCaveat: The range of plausible outcomes for next quarter — read the downside, not the midpoint.
economics.officialCaveat: The hardest series to nowcast — only firm at quarter-end (M3).
economics.officialVintage: Vintage {position} · {pct}% of the quarter's data in
economics.officialSectorsNote: Standardized activity composites (z-scores), not growth rates.
economics.pulseChartAria: Weekly activity pulse, scaled to YoY GDP growth
economics.garChartAria: Range of next-quarter GDP growth outcomes, downside emphasised
economics.recessionChartAria: Recession probability by quarter, with past recessions shaded
economics.disclaimerLead: A fast, consolidated read on where the Portuguese economy is now, built from official high-frequency data — a read of the present, not a forecast.
economics.contributionsMovers: Biggest movers: {down} dragging down, {up} pushing up.
economics.disclaimerFull: Preliminary / tracking. This dashboard describes the current state of the Portuguese economy by combining official high-frequency indicators; it largely co-moves with (and is partly built from) the same activity data published by the Banco de Portugal (notably the Coincident Activity index) and Eurostat. It is a faster, consolidated READ-OUT of that state, NOT a more-accurate forecast of GDP. No tile is statistically shown to beat its same-frequency baseline; treat every number as indicative. The q/q-GDP nowcast in 'official_quarterly' is preliminary and noisy and is only meaningful at quarter-end (M3). All backtests are PSEUDO-real-time (the final revised panel masked by nominal publication lag, NOT true data vintages), so live performance is likely somewhat optimistic; and the effective sample is tiny (≈40 ex-COVID quarters; ~3 recession episodes), so no edge here is statistically significant — this is an honest read-out, not a validated forecasting system. The annual outlook is an arithmetic carryover plus an AR(1) extrapolation that assumes no new shock; its backtested intervals missed in every crisis year.
economics.healthHonesty: A PRESENTATION composite, not a model and not a skill claim. It is a FIXED, transparent monotone blend of three signals the dashboard already shows — the activity pulse (65%), the next-quarter downside (35%), capped by the recession probability — using documented anchor curves, with NO fitting on the score itself. By construction it cannot be more accurate than its parts; its only job is to consolidate them into one honest read. The anchors are tied to PT GDP history (a GFC/Troika/COVID-class contraction maps near 0; trend growth ~50; a boom ~90-100). Inherits every caveat of its inputs: pulse tracks (does not beat) BdP coincident activity, the recession gauge is coincident (timeliness, not anticipation), and the downside band is indicative on a short sample.
economics.pulseHonesty: A coincident activity READ-OUT, not a forecast and not normal-quarter skill. It co-moves with BdP 'Coincident Activity' (live corr 0.69) and is built partly from the same activity data. HONEST pseudo-real-time numbers (validate_pulse.py, 48 expanding refits): the live reading tracks GDP strongly through big swings (incl-COVID level corr 0.80 / change corr 0.77) but WEAKLY on normal quarters (ex-2020+2021 level corr 0.23 / change corr 0.18). It does NOT beat AR(1) incrementally (rel-RMSE ~0.95 ex-COVID, not significant) and only MATCHES (does not beat) the free BdP indicator. ~80% of its movement is forward-filled monthly data, so 'weekly' is cadence, not resolution. Pseudo-real-time masks the final revised panel (not true vintages), so live skill is likely optimistic. Use it to read the activity STATE and catch turns, not as a precise GDP tracker.
economics.recessionCurrentHonesty: Coincident recession-risk READ-OUT (current state), NOT a recession forecast. Brier skill +0.43 vs the climatological base rate — BUT it does NOT beat lag-1 persistence (-0.30): since recession labels are autocorrelated, 'same as last quarter' (which a real-time user has) is the honest baseline. Its genuine value is TIMELINESS (a calibrated read available before the GDP print), not prediction skill. AUC 0.91 is ~matched by a single coincident-activity rule, so it mostly consolidates/calibrates activity it co-moves with. Scored on only ~3 OOS episodes (GFC/Troika/COVID); leave-one-recession-out shows the skill leans on Troika (fragile), and it is NOT statistically significant (DM vs base-rate p~0.35). Pseudo-real-time on revised data, so live performance is likely optimistic.
economics.recessionLeadingHonesty: LEADING recession gauge: probability of recession 2 quarters ahead from survey/credit/financial-stress signals. It beats lag-2 persistence out-of-sample (BSS_vs_persistence +0.32; ex-COVID ~+0.23; leave-one-recession-out +0.23..+0.32, not dependent on any single episode; bootstrap P(BSS>0)~0.93) — modest anticipation value. READ THIS BEFORE TRUSTING THE NUMBER — the 'beats persistence' claim is BASELINE-DEPENDENT: the bar here is y_{t-h} (the label h quarters back, matched to the model's signal vintage). Against the TIGHTEST bar, lag-1 persistence ('same as the last published quarter'), this model LOSES (BSS ~-0.36, ex-COVID ~-0.55). And the lag-h win is LARGELY a forecast-horizon artifact, not unique leading skill: a purely COINCIDENT logistic run h-ahead also beats lag-2/lag-3 persistence (any decent recession reading beats a 2-3-quarter-stale label); the leading signals only edge the coincident model. Other caveats: (1) NOT statistically significant on DM (p~0.19) — n≈4 recession episodes is the binding limit; (2) it cannot anticipate sudden shocks — it predicts the 2020 crash from the pre-pandemic 2019Q4 survey and (correctly) misses it; its edge is gradual onsets (GFC-deepening, Troika-onset) and recovery timing; (3) pseudo-real-time on revised survey data, so live skill is likely optimistic. Bottom line: a reasonable, leak-free anticipation gauge, but its headline skill vs persistence is an artifact of the horizon-matched (stale) baseline — do NOT present it as 'beats same-as-last-quarter'.
economics.sahmHonesty: PT-recalibrated Sahm rule (threshold = 1 std of the PT unemployment-gap distribution, not the US 0.5pp). Caught 3/4 historical PT recessions with 5 false-positive episodes; threshold is in-sample, not out-of-sample optimized.
economics.garHonesty: Risk CONTEXT, not a point forecast. Conditional quantiles of next-quarter GDP growth (Adrian-Boyarchenko-Giannone style), fitted as a conditional Johnson-SU (location+scale respond to stress+activity; skew/tail shape estimated once). The genuine, leak-free, COVID-robust signal is the DOWNSIDE band (q05/q10): through the production backtest rel-pinball 0.75 vs a GARCH/EWMA baseline ex-COVID (0.81 dropping the 2021 rebound too) — a modest improvement over the earlier independent quantile-regression (0.78/0.84, ~3.5% lower pinball) that holds on BOTH panels. The bigger, more robust win is CALIBRATION: tail hit-rates q05 0.050 / q10 0.075 (vs target 0.05/0.10) where QR overshot the 10% tail at 0.11, and PIT-KS p improves 0.13->0.36. The pinball gain ALONE is not bootstrap-significant (p(JSU<QR)≈0.79, 90% CI on the paired difference includes 0); it is adopted because it is consistently better-calibrated with a small pinball gain and no downside, not because it is a proven skill jump. HONEST attribution (ablation): the downside edge over the baseline comes almost entirely from the real-ACTIVITY regressor, NOT from financial stress (activity-only ~0.78 ≈ full; stress-only ~0.91). Financial stress is a correctly-signed conditioner (higher stress lowers q05 via the scale channel) but carries little of the skill — do not bill this as 'financial-conditions-driven'. Still a short sample (~40 ex-COVID-and-rebound quarters); treat it as indicative, not a validated forecasting claim. Lead with the downside; do not read the median as authoritative. Interval coverage (5-95) is ~0.83 vs 0.90 nominal — the band is still modestly too narrow. Pseudo-real-time on revised data, so OOS skill is likely optimistic.
economics.officialHonesty: DEMOTED tile. q/q-GDP is near-white-noise and hard to nowcast. At quarter-end (M3), supply_side_bridge shows rel-RMSE 0.78 vs AR(1) ex-COVID (corr +0.70) — but this is MODEST, NOT statistically significant (DM p≈0.23, bootstrap CI includes 1.0), and partly carried by the 2021 rebound: dropping 2021 too it is rel 0.87 / corr +0.57 (still positive but weaker), and on the last 4 calm years (n=16) the correlation is negative/noise. At M1/M2 it does not beat AR(1). The band is the validated EXPANDING split-conformal interval on the model's own realized M3 errors (COVID/2021-rebound excluded): on the normal-quarter backtest it covers ~82%/93% at nominal 80%/95% with a PIT that is not rejected, replacing the native regression SE which was ~2x too narrow (nominal-80% covered ~56-64%, PIT rejected). Coverage is calibrated for normal quarters; a genuine boom/bust will pierce it. Current vintage is M2: treat this as an early, unreliable placeholder, not an authoritative number.
economics.contributionsHonesty: Attribution of the DFM factor nowcast (the repo's factor-news reference model), not the production headline and not a causal claim. Units are q/q GDP growth (decimal).
economics.pulseTiltNote: Direction-only freshness signal from the weekly index — adds no verified level information.
economics.pulseCombinedNote: freshness overlay — the anchor above is the best level estimate
economics.pulseWeeklyNote: cadence context only — not a level estimate
economics.officialAsymmetric: asymmetric — wider above the point (the model has tended to under-predict growth)
economics.officialWeightsNote: Inverse-MSE weights with a hard AR(1) floor ({model}); the small numbers are each member's own q/q call.
economics.annualEyebrow: How does the year end up?
economics.annualCaveat: Carryover from quarters published through {published}, plus the {nowcast} nowcast and an AR(1) extrapolation — assumes no new shock. Carryover floor: {floor}.
economics.annualChartAria: Simulated distribution of annual GDP growth with institutional forecasts overlaid
economics.annualValidationLine: Replay validation {years}: the 80% interval covered {cov80}% of years ({cov80ex}% ex-COVID); CRPS skill vs climatology +{crps}% (+{crpsEx}% ex-COVID).
economics.trackBacktestTitle: Backtest era — as-if calls, scored against FIRST-published GDP
economics.trackRelFirst: rel. RMSE vs AR(1) · first release
economics.trackTableNote: Below 1.00 = smaller errors than the AR(1) benchmark on the same information set; ex-COVID quarters only. Band hit-rates are indicative (bands were calibrated against the revised target).
economics.labourEyebrow: What are the jobs data saying?
economics.labourNoForecast: A read-out of published labour data — no forecast: next-month unemployment is at its noise floor.
